alternative_translations: wanting things you can't have right now
definition: The ignorant wanting that lunges toward satisfaction through experience, becoming, or non-becoming, including excitement about things and wanting what is not available right now.
notes: OSF house style now prefers ignorant wanting for taṇhā. In this house idiom, excitement about things and wanting things you can't have right now are treated as practical synonyms that expose the restless, mistaken, unsatisfied quality of taṇhā more clearly than the more distant English word craving. Craving remains available when tighter doctrinal comparison is needed, and thirst remains useful for literal or metaphor-sensitive explanation.

context_rules.context: In most doctrinal and practical OSF contexts
In direct practical instruction emphasizing the felt pull toward objects and outcomes
context_rules.notes: Useful when the emphasis is the agitated leaning toward what seems attractive.

context_rules.rendering: wanting things you can't have right now
context_rules.notes: Use when the immediate practical meaning matters more than compact doctrinal style.

context_rules.context: In comparative doctrinal prose engaging established Buddhist-English usage
context_rules.notes: Use when continuity with familiar secondary literature matters.

context_rules.context: When emphasizing the metaphor embedded in the Pāli
context_rules.notes: Acceptable in explanatory prose or special stylistic contexts.
